3A_Bibliographic.citation: Guerrero, M. D., Barnes, J. D., Walsh, J. J., Chaput, J.-P., Tremblay, M. S., & Goldfield, G. S. (2019). 24-Hour movement behaviors and impulsivity. Pediatrics, 144(3), e20190187.

3B_Sample_Data.abcd_release: First (baseline) cross-sectional curated release of the ABCD Study data set
3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: We used the first (baseline) cross-sectional curated release of the ABCD Study data set, which comprised 4524 children aged 8 to 11 years.

3C_Exposure.exposure_variable: Meeting 24-hour movement behavior recommendation combinations: physical activity (PA >=60 min/day), screen time (ST <=2 hrs/day), sleep (9-11 hrs/night), and their combinations
3C_Exposure.exposure_instrument: Youth Screen Time Survey (12 items) for ST; Youth Risk Behavior Survey for PA; Parent Sleep Disturbance Scale for Children for sleep
3C_Exposure.modalities: total recreational screen time (watching shows/movies, texting)
3C_Exposure.operationalization: Categorical (8 possible combinations of meeting/not meeting PA, ST, and sleep recommendations; reference = meeting no recommendations)
3C_Exposure.time_frame: Typical weekday and weekend day for ST; past 7 days for PA; most nights for sleep
3C_Exposure.reporter: Self-report (youth) for ST and PA; parent-report for sleep
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: Recreational ST was measured by using the Youth Screen Time Survey (12 items), wherein children were asked to report the number of hours spent engaged in screen-based activities (eg, watching shows and movies, texting) on a typical weekday and weekend day. Daily recreational ST was calculated by taking a weighted average of the weekday and weekend ST items... Participants were classified into 1 of 8 possible movement behavior recommendation combinations.
3C_Exposure.notes: Screen time is one of three 24-hour movement behaviors examined as part of meeting/not meeting guidelines.

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Dimensions of impulsivity (UPPS-P: positive urgency, negative urgency, perseverance; BIS/BAS: BIS, BAS drive, BAS reward responsiveness, BAS fun-seeking; and delay-discounting task)
3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: UPPS-P Impulsive Behavior Scale, BIS/BAS Scale, cash choice delay-discounting task
3D_Outcome.base_rate_if_binary: N/A - continuous outcomes (except delay-discounting which is binary smaller/larger reward)
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: Dimensions of impulsivity served as the outcome variables and were measured by using 3 child-reported assessments: UPPS-P Impulsive Behavior Scale, Behavioral Inhibition System (BIS)/Behavioral Activation System (BAS) Scale, and cash choice delay-discounting task.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Structural equation modeling (SEM) with confirmatory factor analysis; logistic regression for delay-discounting
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: sex, BMI, family income, parental education, race and ethnicity
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: Structural equation modeling was used to evaluate both the measurement models and the hypothesized structural model. In the hypothesized structural model, impulsivity dimensions were modeled as latent variables, and movement behavior combinations and the delay-discounting task were modeled as observed variables. Covariates (ie, sex, BMI, family income, parental education, and race and ethnicity) were included in the model... All analyses were conducted by using Mplus 8.2. Missing data (<1%) were replaced by using multiple imputation. Robust full-information maximum likelihood estimation was used.

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Only ST recommendation met (vs. no recommendation met)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Table 3: Only ST, Positive urgency beta = -.08, SE = 0.02, P < .001, 95% CI -0.12 to -0.04

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Only ST recommendation met (vs. no recommendation met)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Table 3: Only ST, Negative urgency beta = -.13, SE = 0.02, P < .001, 95% CI -0.17 to -0.08

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Only ST recommendation met (vs. no recommendation met)
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Standardized path coefficient (high score = low impulsivity)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Table 3: Only ST, Perseverance beta = .10, SE = 0.02, P < .001, 95% CI 0.06 to 0.14

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: ST + sleep recommendations met (vs. no recommendation met)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Table 3: ST + sleep, Positive urgency beta = -.12, SE = 0.02, P < .001, 95% CI -0.16 to -0.08

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: ST + sleep recommendations met (vs. no recommendation met)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Table 3: ST + sleep, Negative urgency beta = -.15, SE = 0.02, P < .001, 95% CI -0.20 to -0.10

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: PA + ST + sleep (all recommendations met, vs. no recommendation met)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Table 3: PA + ST + sleep, Positive urgency beta = -.09, SE = 0.02, P < .001, 95% CI -0.12 to -0.05

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Only ST recommendation met (vs. no recommendation met)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Table 4: Only ST, BIS beta = -.05, SE = 0.03, P = .045, 95% CI -0.10 to 0.00

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: Only ST recommendation met (vs. no recommendation met)
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Table 4: Only ST, BAS drive beta = -.08, SE = 0.02, P < .001, 95% CI -0.11 to -0.05

3F_Effect_Sizes.predictor: ST + sleep recommendations met (vs. no recommendation met)
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Odds ratio for choosing smaller, sooner reward (lower = less impulsive)

3G_Reporting_Quality.abstract_significance_verbatim: Meeting the ST and sleep recommendations was associated with less impulsive behaviors on all dimensions of impulsivity
3G_Reporting_Quality.small_es_verbatim: The models explained 3.4% to 9.7% of the variance of impulsivity scores.
3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: The standardized betas are small (mostly 0.05-0.15 range). The paper reports R-squared values of 3.4%-9.7% but these include all covariates and all movement behavior combinations, not just screen time. The paper does not discuss how much of the variance is uniquely attributable to screen time.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: Our findings have important implications for pediatricians, psychiatrists, educators, parents, and policy makers as they suggest that strategies to limit recreational ST while simultaneously promoting early, routine bedtimes and more sleep may enhance the treatment and prevention of impulsivity-related psychiatric disorders.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.modifiable_factor_verbatim: identifying modifiable determinants of impulsivity can inform targets to enhance the treatment and prevention of impulse control-related psychiatric disorders
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion emphasizes that the combination of meeting screen time and sleep recommendations had the strongest associations with reduced impulsivity across most dimensions, whereas physical activity provided limited additional benefit. It discusses neurobiological mechanisms linking screen time to impulsivity via dopamine reward pathways and diminished self-regulatory capacity. The paper frames findings as supporting clinical guidelines to limit recreational screen time, despite the small effect sizes (standardized betas of 0.05-0.15 and R-squared of 3-10%).
